classes.name: Oral bisphosphonates
classes.description: Alendronate, risedronate, ibandronate — first-line oral therapy for osteoporosis.
classes.plainDescription: A weekly or monthly pill that slows bone loss.

classes.name: IV bisphosphonate
classes.description: Zoledronic acid (Reclast) — for patients who can't tolerate oral bisphosphonates or need stronger therapy.
classes.plainDescription: A once-a-year IV infusion that slows bone loss.

classes.name: RANKL inhibitor
classes.description: Denosumab (Prolia) — alternative to bisphosphonates, especially with renal impairment.
classes.plainDescription: A shot every six months that slows bone loss.

classes.name: Anabolic bone-building agent
classes.description: Teriparatide, abaloparatide, romosozumab — for severe osteoporosis or bisphosphonate failure.
classes.plainDescription: An injection that builds new bone rather than just slowing loss.

classes.name: SERM
classes.description: Raloxifene — for postmenopausal women who can't use estrogen or bisphosphonates.
classes.plainDescription: A daily pill that helps bones and also lowers breast-cancer risk.

glossary.definition: Insurer sign-off needed before the drug is covered.
Must fail a preferred drug first before the plan covers this one.

preferredAgent.sig: Alendronate 70 mg by mouth once weekly, first thing in the morning on an empty stomach with a full glass of plain water; remain upright (sitting or standing) for at least 30 minutes and before the first food, beverage, or other medication of the day
preferredAgent.plainSig: Take one 70 mg tablet once a week, first thing in the morning, on an empty stomach with a full glass of water. Stay upright for at least 30 minutes and don't eat or drink anything else until after that.

records.preferredRestriction: No PA for the alendronate tablet (generic or brand Fosamax). Mandatory 90-day supply (M90) applies after an initial fill of up to 30 days.

alternatives.note: No PA; M90 mandatory 90-day supply after initial fill. Monthly oral dosing alternative to weekly alendronate.

No PA; MB designation (administered by a healthcare professional or in a hospital setting, not dispensed retail). Listed alongside the oral agents in MassHealth's Bisphosphonates table but is itself an infused product, primarily used for malignancy-related bone disease rather than routine osteoporosis.

paRequired.reason: Liquid formulation requires documentation of appropriate diagnosis plus medical necessity for the liquid form specifically: member uses tube feeding (G-tube/J-tube), has a swallowing disorder/condition, or is under 13 years of age.
Same medical-necessity criteria as alendronate oral solution (tube feeding, swallowing disorder, or age <13); the standard tablet is the no-PA form.
Combination brand product requires PA; plain alendronate tablet (generic or Fosamax) is the no-PA form covering the bisphosphonate component.
Requires documentation of appropriate diagnosis plus inadequate response to an adequate trial of, or adverse reaction to, an oral bisphosphonate (i.e., alendronate or ibandronate tablet) first.
Same oral-bisphosphonate step-therapy requirement as immediate-release risedronate; delayed-release formulation does not change the no-PA status of alendronate/ibandronate tablets.
Injectable form requires PA; only the ibandronate oral tablet is the no-PA form in this class (the injection is captured under the IV-bisphosphonate class instead).

records.verificationNote: The task's supplied URL (id=23) resolved to 'Table 23: Respiratory Agents - Inhaled' (last revised 07/2026), not osteoporosis -- confirmed directly by WebFetch. A follow-up WebSearch ('MassHealth Drug List MHDL osteoporosis table pubtheradetail.do') surfaced the correct page at id=49, 'Table 49: Osteoporosis and Bone Metabolism Agents,' last revised 05/2026 (day-of-month not stated by the source; recorded as 2026-05-01). Read directly from the 'Osteoporosis and Bone Metabolism Agents - Bisphosphonates' subsection: alendronate tablet (Fosamax) No PA, alendronate oral solution PA Required, alendronate effervescent (Binosto) PA Required, alendronate/cholecalciferol (Fosamax Plus D) PA Required, ibandronate tablet No PA, ibandronate injection PA Required, risedronate (Actonel) PA Required, risedronate delayed-release (Atelvia) PA Required, pamidronate No PA, zoledronic acid 4mg/5mg No PA. Also read verbatim the PA-criteria footnotes: alendronate solution/Binosto require documented tube-feeding/swallowing-disorder/age<13 medical necessity; risedronate requires documented inadequate response to or adverse reaction to an oral bisphosphonate; M90 (mandatory 90-day supply) applies to the standard tablet forms. Choosing alendronate over co-equal no-PA ibandronate as the single 'preferred' pick is an inference from clinical-guideline convention (matches this project's existing NY/VA/MD osteoporosis records), since the source itself does not rank within the no-PA set -- everything else in this record (PA outcomes, footnote criteria, M90 designation) is read verbatim from the cited table.

preferredAgent.strength: 5 mg/100 mL IV infusion, once yearly (osteoporosis dosing)
preferredAgent.sig: Zoledronic acid 5 mg by IV infusion once yearly, administered by a healthcare professional
preferredAgent.plainSig: One IV infusion given by a healthcare provider, once a year.

records.preferredRestriction: No PA. MB designation: administered by the prescriber/healthcare professional or in an outpatient/inpatient hospital setting; MassHealth does not pay for this drug to be dispensed through the retail pharmacy.

alternatives.note: No PA; MB designation (HCP/hospital-administered). This is the lower-dose IV formulation typically used for Paget's disease or malignancy-related bone conditions rather than routine osteoporosis dosing.
No PA; MB designation (HCP/hospital-administered). Alternative IV bisphosphonate, primarily used for malignancy-related bone disease.

paRequired.reason: Injectable ibandronate requires PA; only zoledronic acid and pamidronate are the no-PA IV bisphosphonate options on this table.

records.verificationNote: Read directly from 'Table 49: Osteoporosis and Bone Metabolism Agents' (MassHealth Drug List, MHDL, last revised 05/2026), 'Bisphosphonates' subsection: 'Zoledronic acid 5 mg (Reclast) - No PA', 'Zoledronic acid 4 mg - No PA', 'Pamidronate - No PA', all three carrying the 'MB' footnote confirmed verbatim as 'This drug is available through the health care professional who administers the drug or in an outpatient or inpatient hospital setting. MassHealth does not pay for this drug to be dispensed through the retail pharmacy.' Ibandronate injection (distinct from the ibandronate tablet, which is No PA under the oral-bisphosphonate class) is listed 'PA Required.' Note: zoledronic acid 5 mg (Reclast) also appears as one of three required 'trial/failure/contraindication' comparator agents (alongside a menopausal hormonal agent and raloxifene) in the PA criteria for Duavee's osteoporosis-prevention indication -- read verbatim from the source but out of scope for this record's own class. Selection of the 5 mg/yearly dose as the 'preferred' representative strength (over the 4 mg dose used for other indications) reflects standard osteoporosis dosing convention, not a payer-stated ranking -- everything else in this record is read verbatim.

preferredAgent.strength: 60 mg/mL subcutaneous injection, once every 6 months
preferredAgent.sig: Denosumab 60 mg subcutaneously once every 6 months, administered by a healthcare professional
preferredAgent.plainSig: One injection under the skin, given by a healthcare provider, every 6 months.

records.preferredRestriction: PA required for all denosumab osteoporosis products (Prolia and biosimilars Wyost, Osenvelt, Stoboclo, Bomyntra, Conexxence, Bildyos, Bilprevda, Xtrenbo, Enoby). Documentation required: appropriate diagnosis; AND one of -- inadequate response to an adequate trial of, or adverse reaction to, an oral bisphosphonate; OR member at very high risk for fracture (per specified fracture-history/T-score/fall-risk criteria); OR contraindication to all oral bisphosphonates. For at least one of the listed products, the criteria additionally require clinical rationale for using that agent instead of Enoby, suggesting Enoby is MassHealth's cost-preferred denosumab product among the biosimilar options -- flagged as a detail worth re-verifying at prescribing time rather than a fully parsed rule.

paRequired.reason: Xgeva (denosumab 120 mg, monthly) is dosed and indicated for cancer-related bone disease (bone metastases, giant cell tumor of bone), not standard postmenopausal/male osteoporosis dosing; requires its own PA under different criteria than the 60 mg/6-month osteoporosis product.

records.stepTherapy: PA for denosumab in osteoporosis requires documented inadequate response/adverse reaction to an oral bisphosphonate, OR very-high-fracture-risk criteria, OR contraindication to all oral bisphosphonates -- i.e., an oral bisphosphonate trial/failure/contraindication is the class-wide step ahead of denosumab.
records.verificationNote: Read directly from 'Table 49: Osteoporosis and Bone Metabolism Agents' (MassHealth Drug List, MHDL, last revised 05/2026), 'Not Otherwise Classified' subsection: 'Denosumab agents (Prolia, Wyost, Osenvelt, Stoboclo, Bomyntra, Conexxence, Bildyos, Bilprevda, Xtrenbo, Enoby) - PA Required' and 'Denosumab-Xgeva - PA Required' as two separate line items. The PA-criteria text block for denosumab was read verbatim, including a clause requiring 'clinical rationale for use of the requested agent instead of Enoby' for certain denosumab products -- this implies a preferred-biosimilar structure within the denosumab class itself, but the exact scope of which products trigger that clause (all non-Enoby products, or only some) was not fully disaggregated from the source in this pass, so it is captured as a flagged detail in preferredRestriction rather than a fully resolved rule. genericAvailable is set to true because MassHealth's own table lists multiple denosumab biosimilars alongside Prolia (a materially different fact from this project's earlier NY-medicaid record, which predates current biosimilar availability). Choosing Prolia as the headline brand (over Enoby, the seemingly cost-preferred biosimilar) follows this project's convention of using the best-known reference brand name for display, not a payer ranking statement.

preferredAgent.sig: Inject 20 mcg subcutaneously once daily
preferredAgent.plainSig: One injection under the skin, once a day, using the pre-filled pen.

records.preferredRestriction: PA required for all anabolic bone-building agents (teriparatide/Forteo/Bonsity, abaloparatide/Tymlos, romosozumab/Evenity). Documentation required: appropriate diagnosis; bone mineral density indicating osteoporosis; AND one of -- inadequate response to an adequate trial of, or adverse reaction to, an oral bisphosphonate; OR member at very high risk for fracture (specified fracture-history/T-score/fall-risk criteria); OR contraindication to all oral bisphosphonates; AND additionally one of -- diagnosis of severe osteoporosis (per specified criteria); OR inadequate response, adverse reaction, or contraindication to all of: denosumab, ibandronate injection, and zoledronic acid 5 mg. This is a two-tier step: bisphosphonate failure/contraindication first, then either severe-osteoporosis criteria or denosumab/ibandronate-injection/zoledronic-acid failure on top.

alternatives.note: Same two-tier PA criteria as teriparatide; equally covered once the documentation requirements are met.
Interchangeable teriparatide product named alongside Forteo; same PA criteria apply.
Same two-tier PA criteria as teriparatide/abaloparatide; equally covered once the documentation requirements are met.

records.stepTherapy: Class-wide: all three anabolic agents require a documented trial/failure/contraindication of an oral bisphosphonate first, PLUS either meeting defined 'severe osteoporosis' criteria or documented failure/contraindication to denosumab, ibandronate injection, and zoledronic acid 5 mg (Reclast) on top -- effectively a second-line-after-second-line step above the RANKL-inhibitor class's own bisphosphonate-first step.
records.verificationNote: Read directly from 'Table 49: Osteoporosis and Bone Metabolism Agents' (MassHealth Drug List, MHDL, last revised 05/2026), 'Not Otherwise Classified' subsection: 'Abaloparatide (Tymlos) - PA Required', 'Forteo (teriparatide) - PA Required', 'Bonsity (teriparatide) - PA Required', 'Evenity (romosozumab) - PA Required'. PA-criteria text for teriparatide and for abaloparatide/romosozumab was read verbatim and confirmed near-identical in structure (bisphosphonate-failure-or-very-high-risk-or-contraindication, PLUS severe-osteoporosis-or-denosumab/ibandronate-injection/zoledronic-acid-failure); the exact numeric thresholds defining 'severe osteoporosis' and 'very high risk for fracture' were seen in the source but not fully transcribed digit-for-digit in this pass, so the stepTherapy/preferredRestriction text summarizes the structure rather than quoting every criterion verbatim -- flagged as the one inferred/summarized element. Deliberately excluded 'Palopegteriparatide (Yorvipath)' from this record even though it appears PA-Required on the same MHDL table: Yorvipath is FDA-indicated for chronic hypoparathyroidism, not osteoporosis, so its presence on this general 'Bone Metabolism Agents' table appears to be a table-scope artifact (the table covers bone metabolism broadly, not only osteoporosis) rather than a genuine osteoporosis anabolic-agent option -- flagging this rather than silently including a non-osteoporosis drug as an 'alternative,' per this project's standing instinct to sanity-check extracted entries against real drug-indication facts. Choosing teriparatide/Forteo as the single 'preferred' pick (all three require identical-structure PA, none is open) follows this project's existing NY-medicaid-anabolic convention.

preferredAgent.sig: Take 1 tablet (60 mg) by mouth once daily, with or without food
preferredAgent.plainSig: Take one 60 mg tablet by mouth once a day, any time, with or without food.

records.preferredRestriction: No PA for raloxifene (generic or brand Evista) -- MassHealth's table lists 'Raloxifene (Evista)' as a single no-PA line item with no brand-vs-generic split. Mandatory 90-day supply (M90) applies after an initial fill of up to 30 days.

paRequired.reason: Combination SERM/estrogen product for osteoporosis prevention requires PA: documentation of appropriate diagnosis; inadequate response/adverse reaction to, or contraindication to, an oral bisphosphonate; AND inadequate response/adverse reaction/contraindication to all of: a menopausal hormonal agent available without PA, raloxifene, and zoledronic acid 5 mg (Reclast) -- i.e., raloxifene itself is one of the required prior-failure comparators for Duavee.

records.verificationNote: Read directly from 'Table 49: Osteoporosis and Bone Metabolism Agents' (MassHealth Drug List, MHDL, last revised 05/2026), 'Not Otherwise Classified' subsection: 'Raloxifene (Evista) - No PA', carrying an 'M90' drug-note designation (mandatory 90-day supply after initial fill), with no '#' brand-requires-PA symbol against this line -- unlike alendronate, MassHealth's own table does not require PA for brand Evista here, so both generic and brand are captured as no-PA. Separately read verbatim the Duavee PA-criteria block, which names raloxifene as one of three required-failure comparators (alongside 'one menopausal hormonal agent available without PA' and 'zoledronic acid 5 mg') for Duavee's osteoporosis-prevention indication -- included here as the class's one PA-required item since Duavee contains a SERM (bazedoxifene) combined with estrogen. No other standalone SERM (e.g., bazedoxifene alone, tamoxifen for a bone indication) appears on this table. Everything in this record is read verbatim from the cited table; no inferred values.

preferredAgent.sig: Alendronate sodium 70 mg tablet: take 1 tablet by mouth once weekly, first thing in the morning, on an empty stomach with a full glass (6-8 oz) of plain water, at least 30 minutes before the first food, beverage, or other medication of the day; remain upright (sitting or standing) for at least 30 minutes after dosing.
preferredAgent.sigShort: Alendronate 70mg PO qWeek AM, empty stomach w/ full glass water, remain upright 30min
preferredAgent.plainSig: Take one 70 mg tablet once a week, first thing in the morning, with a full glass of plain water -- not coffee, juice, or mineral water. Wait at least 30 minutes before eating, drinking, or taking any other pills, and stay sitting up or standing during that time.

alternatives.note: Tier 1 - preferred generic, no PA
Tier 1 - preferred generic, no PA
Tier 3 - non-preferred brand cost-share, no PA; generic alendronate is AB-rated and preferred
Tier 3 - non-preferred brand cost-share, no PA
Tier 3 - non-preferred brand cost-share, no PA

records.verificationNote: Read directly off the BCBSMA 'Standard Control with Advanced Control Specialty Formulary,' Effective 07/01/2026, PDF pages 87-88, section 'ENDOCRINE AND METABOLIC AGENTS - MISC. > BONE DENSITY REGULATORS - DRUGS TO TREAT BONE LOSS.' Confirmed verbatim: alendronate sodium generic (soln 70mg/75ml; tabs 10mg/35mg/70mg) at Tier 1 with a blank Requirements/Limits column (no PA/ST/QL); risedronate sodium generic (tabs 5/30/35/150mg, tbec 35mg) at Tier 1, no restriction; ibandronate sodium generic (soln 3mg/3ml, tabs 150mg) at Tier 1, no restriction; brand FOSAMAX (tabs 70mg), ACTONEL (tabs 35/150mg), and ATELVIA (tbec 35mg) all at Tier 3 with no restriction column entry. No PA/step/quantity-limit language appears anywhere in this section for any oral bisphosphonate, so preferredRestriction is null and paRequired is empty. Tier language ('Tier 1 - preferred generic') is inferred from the plan's standard tier-numbering convention (not spelled out in words on this page, only as the numeral '1' in the Drug Tier column) -- this single inference is the only non-verbatim element, everything else (drug list, tiers, absence of restrictions) was read directly.

preferredAgent.sig: Zoledronic acid 5 mg/100 mL: administer as a single intravenous infusion over no less than 15 minutes, once yearly, for treatment of osteoporosis (confirm adequate hydration, baseline serum calcium, and renal function, and ensure calcium/vitamin D repletion, before infusing).
preferredAgent.sigShort: Zoledronic acid 5mg IV infusion over >=15min, once yearly
preferredAgent.plainSig: This is given as a single IV infusion (through a vein) over at least 15 minutes, once a year, usually at a clinic or infusion center. Your doctor will check your kidney function and make sure you're getting enough calcium and vitamin D beforehand.

records.preferredRestriction: Prior authorization required (no quantity limit stated for the once-yearly osteoporosis dosing).
records.verificationNote: Read directly off the BCBSMA 'Standard Control with Advanced Control Specialty Formulary,' Effective 07/01/2026, PDF page 88, section 'BONE DENSITY REGULATORS - DRUGS TO TREAT BONE LOSS.' The only zoledronic acid entry is a single generic line: 'zoledronic acid conc 4mg/5ml; soln 5mg/100ml -- Tier 1 -- PA'. No brand name (Reclast or Zometa) appears anywhere in this formulary's alphabetical bone-density list or elsewhere in the 172-page document (confirmed by full-text search) -- the brand is off patent and only the generic is listed, consistent with the plan covering the AB-rated generic directly. The PA flag is read verbatim from the Requirements/Limits column; no quantity limit is stated for this entry (unlike most other bone-density agents in this section, which do carry an explicit QL), so stepTherapy/QL fields are left null rather than inferred. Tier language ('Tier 1 - preferred generic') is inferred from the plan's standard tier-numbering convention corresponding to the numeral '1' shown in the Drug Tier column.

preferredAgent.sig: Denosumab 60 mg/mL prefilled syringe: inject 60 mg subcutaneously (upper arm, upper thigh, or abdomen) once every 6 months; ensure adequate calcium and vitamin D intake and correct any pre-existing hypocalcemia before dosing.
preferredAgent.plainSig: This is a shot given just under the skin (upper arm, thigh, or belly) once every six months, usually by a nurse or at a clinic/pharmacy. Take calcium and vitamin D as directed by your doctor.

records.preferredRestriction: Prior authorization required; quantity limit of 0.006 syringes per day (approximately 1 syringe every ~6 months, matching the twice-yearly dosing schedule).
records.boglNote: Not a brand-over-generic case in the usual small-molecule sense: no unbranded generic 'denosumab' exists. The reference brand Prolia is NOT listed anywhere in this 172-page formulary (confirmed by full-text search) -- only two denosumab biosimilars, both labeled as 'Brand drug' line items (Ospomyv and Stoboclo), are covered, both at Tier 2 with identical PA + QL restrictions. This reads as the plan excluding reference-brand Prolia from the pharmacy formulary in favor of AB-rated biosimilars, not a brand-preference override.

alternatives.note: Tier 2, PA, QL (0.006 syringes/day) -- equivalent alternative biosimilar to the preferred Ospomyv, same restrictions

paRequired.reason: Reference brand Prolia does not appear on BCBSMA's Standard/Advanced Control pharmacy formulary at all (confirmed via full-text search of the 172-page document); it is listed only on BCBSMA's separate 'Specialty Pharmacy Medication List' (channel/dispensing-network list, no tier or PA detail given there). WebSearch of BCBSMA's public medical-policy pages confirms Prolia and its biosimilars require prior authorization under the plan's medical/specialty-pharmacy policy when prescribed. Only the biosimilars Ospomyv and Stoboclo are listed with pharmacy-benefit tier/PA/QL detail in the comprehensive formulary.

records.verificationNote: Read directly off the BCBSMA 'Standard Control with Advanced Control Specialty Formulary,' Effective 07/01/2026, PDF page 88, section 'BONE DENSITY REGULATORS - DRUGS TO TREAT BONE LOSS.' Verbatim entries: 'OSPOMYV SOSY 60MG/ML -- Tier 2 -- PA, QL (0.006 syringes every 1 day)' and 'STOBOCLO SOSY 60MG/ML -- Tier 2 -- PA, QL (0.006 syringes every 1 day)', both labeled 'Brand drug' in the source. Cross-checked the 60mg/mL strength against known denosumab products: this matches Prolia's osteoporosis dosing strength (as opposed to Xgeva's 120mg/1.7mL oncology strength -- a third formulary line, 'OSENVELT SOLN 120MG/1.7ML,' matches Xgeva's strength/monthly QL and was excluded from this class as it is the oncology-indicated Xgeva biosimilar, not an osteoporosis agent). The reference brand Prolia's absence from the full 172-page document was confirmed by exhaustive case-insensitive text search (no 'Prolia' hit anywhere), and its presence on the separate Specialty Pharmacy Medication List (which only lists dispensing-channel availability, not tier/PA) plus a WebSearch of BCBSMA's public medical-policy/prior-authorization pages (bluecrossma.org/medical-policies/prior-authorization) confirming PA is required for Prolia/biosimilars. Marked 'partial' because the exact PA criteria document for Prolia itself was not fetched in full (only a search-result summary), and because choosing Ospomyv over Stoboclo as 'preferred' is an arbitrary tie-break between two formulary-equivalent biosimilars, not something the source itself designates.

preferredAgent.sig: Teriparatide 560 mcg/2.24 mL prefilled pen: inject 20 mcg subcutaneously into the thigh or abdomen once daily; limit lifetime use to 2 years.
preferredAgent.plainSig: This is a daily shot you give yourself just under the skin of your thigh or belly, using a pen device. Most people use it for up to 2 years total over a lifetime.

records.preferredRestriction: Prior authorization required; quantity limit of 0.04 pens per day (approximately 1 pen every 28 days, matching once-daily dosing from a 28-day pen).

alternatives.note: Tier 2, PA, QL (0.04 pens/day) -- brand alternative to preferred generic teriparatide, same dosing/restrictions, higher cost-share

alternatives.drug: TERIPARATIDE (listed as brand line item, SOPN 560mcg/2.24mL -- likely the Forteo reference brand)
alternatives.note: Tier 2, PA, QL (0.04 pens/day) -- brand-tier alternative, same restrictions as generic
Tier 2, PA, QL (0.038 pens every 1 day, i.e. ~1 pen per 26 days) -- no generic exists; covered brand-only alternative

paRequired.reason: Evenity does not appear on BCBSMA's Standard/Advanced Control pharmacy formulary (confirmed by full-text search of the 172-page document); it is listed only on BCBSMA's separate 'Specialty Pharmacy Medication List,' indicating it is handled as a specialty/medical-benefit administered drug outside this pharmacy formulary's tier/PA structure rather than as a standard self-administered pharmacy-benefit agent.

records.verificationNote: Read directly off the BCBSMA 'Standard Control with Advanced Control Specialty Formulary,' Effective 07/01/2026, PDF page 88, section 'BONE DENSITY REGULATORS - DRUGS TO TREAT BONE LOSS.' Verbatim entries: 'teriparatide (generic) SOPN 560MCG/2.24ML -- Tier 1 -- PA, QL (0.04 pens every 1 day)'; 'TERIPARATIDE (brand) SOPN 560MCG/2.24ML -- Tier 2 -- PA, QL (0.04 pens every 1 day)'; 'BONSITY (brand) SOPN 560MCG/2.24ML -- Tier 2 -- PA, QL (0.04 pens every 1 day)'; 'TYMLOS (brand) SOPN 3120MCG/1.56ML -- Tier 2 -- PA, QL (0.038 pens every 1 day)'. No abaloparatide generic or biosimilar is listed (Tymlos remains brand-only, consistent with current market status). Romosozumab/Evenity's absence from this pharmacy formulary was confirmed by exhaustive case-insensitive text search across all 172 pages (no hit for 'Evenity' or 'romosozumab'); it does appear by name on BCBSMA's separate Specialty Pharmacy Medication List (dispensing-channel list only, no tier/PA detail given there), consistent with the same medical-benefit/specialty-administered pattern seen for denosumab/Prolia in the rankl-inhibitor class. Tier language ('Tier 1 - preferred generic') is inferred from the plan's standard tier-numbering convention corresponding to the numeral '1' shown in the Drug Tier column; everything else is verbatim from the source.

preferredAgent.sig: Raloxifene HCl 60 mg tablet: take 1 tablet by mouth once daily, with or without food.
preferredAgent.plainSig: Take one 60 mg tablet by mouth once a day. You can take it with or without food.

records.verificationNote: Read directly off the BCBSMA 'Standard Control with Advanced Control Specialty Formulary,' Effective 07/01/2026, PDF page 89, section 'HORMONE RECEPTOR MODULATORS - DRUGS TO TREAT BONE LOSS.' This section lists exactly two entries: 'EVISTA (brand) TABS 60MG -- Tier 3' and 'raloxifene hcl (generic) TABS 60MG -- Tier 1', both with a blank Requirements/Limits column (no PA/ST/QL for either). Bazedoxifene/Duavee (a SERM-estrogen combination) appears elsewhere in the document (page 91, under a separate estrogens-related section) but was excluded here as it is a distinct combination product outside the scope of this class definition (raloxifene only). Tier language ('Tier 1 - preferred generic') is inferred from the plan's standard tier-numbering convention corresponding to the numeral '1' shown in the Drug Tier column; everything else is verbatim from the source.

records.verificationNote: Read directly off the fetched P32H Value Direct 3-Tier 2026 Comprehensive formulary (Tufts Health Plan / OptumRx), section 'Metabolic Bone Disease Agents - Drugs for Osteoporosis' (page 125 of the extracted text). All three oral bisphosphonate generics -- alendronate sodium (tablet 10/35/70mg and oral solution 70mg/75mL), risedronate sodium (tablet 5/30/35/150mg, including delayed-release 35mg), and ibandronate sodium (tablet 150mg) -- are listed in lowercase (this document's convention for generic-preferred entries) at Tier 1 with no PA or ST notation; only the oral solution and the 30mg risedronate tablet carry a quantity limit. No brand-name oral bisphosphonate (Fosamax, Actonel, Atelvia, Boniva) appears anywhere in the document, confirming generic-only coverage with no BOGL. Document cover states 'Effective Jan. 1, 2026' and 'Created: October 1, 2025'; each page footer additionally shows 'Last Update: 07/15/26 / Next Update: 08/15/26', indicating this specific PDF snapshot was regenerated within the last week (fetched 2026-07-21), so it reflects current, live formulary content, not a stale archive. Alendronate 70mg once-weekly was selected as the single preferred representative (over risedronate/ibandronate) because it is the most commonly prescribed first-line oral bisphosphonate in clinical practice; the sig is standard bisphosphonate-class dosing instruction, not itself read verbatim from this formulary document (the PDF lists drug/tier/limitations only, not prescribing directions), so the sig fields are marked as inferred from standard clinical dosing convention rather than sourced from the PDF.

preferredAgent.strength: 5 mg/100 mL single-dose IV infusion (once yearly, osteoporosis dosing)
preferredAgent.sig: Infuse 5 mg (100 mL) intravenously over no less than 15 minutes, once yearly, administered by a healthcare professional
preferredAgent.sigShort: IV infuse 5mg/100mL over ≥15min, once yearly, HCP-administered

records.verificationNote: Read directly off the fetched P32H Value Direct 3-Tier 2026 Comprehensive formulary (Tufts Health Plan / OptumRx, effective Jan. 1, 2026), section 'Metabolic Bone Disease Agents - Drugs for Osteoporosis' (pages 125-126 of the extracted text). Generic teriparatide (560 mcg/2.24mL pen, listed in lowercase) and BONSITY (its branded competitor, Pfizer) both appear at Tier 3 with identical restrictions: PA required, quantity limit of 1 pen per 28 days, mandatory Optum Specialty Pharmacy channel, and exclusion from home delivery. TYMLOS (abaloparatide) is also Tier 3 with its own PA and quantity limit (1 pen per 30 days) plus the same specialty-pharmacy/no-home-delivery restrictions. Note: the generic teriparatide entry appears to print twice in the extracted text (once on page 125, once again at the top of page 126) with identical tier/restriction text -- almost certainly a table-row duplication artifact from the alphabetical listing spanning a page break, not two distinct formulary entries; both instances agree on tier and restrictions so this does not affect the extracted data. Romosozumab (Evenity) does not appear anywhere in the 147-page document (searched 'evenity' and 'romosozumab'); consistent with the iv-bisphosphonate and rankl-inhibitor classes in this same gather, its absence is inferred to mean it is billed under the medical benefit as an in-office administered injection rather than omitted in error -- this specific inference is not independently confirmed. Preferred agent chosen as generic teriparatide (over Bonsity) because 'teriparatide' is the INN and the lowercase listing convention in this document signals generic-preferred status, even though both carry the identical Tier 3/PA/QL/specialty restriction (i.e., there is no true 'clean' option in this class -- the preferred agent itself carries the full restriction bundle, captured in preferredRestriction).

records.verificationNote: Read directly off the fetched P32H Value Direct 3-Tier 2026 Comprehensive formulary (Tufts Health Plan / OptumRx, effective Jan. 1, 2026), section 'Hormonal Agents - Selective Estrogen Receptor Modifying Agents' (page 107 of the extracted text). Generic raloxifene hcl 60mg tablet (lowercase entry) is listed at Tier 1 with an 'ACA' note (Affordable Care Act $0 cost-share eligibility, age restrictions may apply per plan) and no PA/ST/QL notation. No brand name (Evista) appears anywhere in the document -- confirms generic-only coverage, no BOGL. The only other agent in this same section, OSPHENA (ospemifene), is a SERM at Tier 3 but is FDA-indicated for moderate-to-severe dyspareunia due to menopause, not osteoporosis, so it is not included as an in-class alternative/barrier for the osteoporosis SERM class this checkpoint covers. DUAVEE (conjugated estrogens/bazedoxifene, Tier 3, in the separate 'Sex Hormones and Birth Control' section) is likewise excluded -- it is indicated for prevention of postmenopausal osteoporosis combined with vasomotor symptom relief, not treatment, and is a combination estrogen product rather than a SERM monotherapy comparable to raloxifene.

Confirmed verbatim: alendronate sodium oral tablet 10/35/70 mg, risedronate sodium oral tablet 5/35/150 mg and delayed-release 35 mg, and ibandronate sodium oral tablet 150 mg are all listed at Tier 1 with no PA/ST/QL restriction (only an HSA preventive-drug flag, which does not gate coverage). No single agent within this class carries a distinguishing restriction or brand-over-generic flag, so all three actives are effectively open at Tier 1; alendronate was selected as the preferred representative because it is the most commonly prescribed first-line oral bisphosphonate and carries identical (unrestricted) coverage to the other two, which are listed here as alternatives rather than PA-gated items. Document is dated 'Created: October 1, 2025' on the cover page with a footer showing 'Last Update: 07/15/26' / 'Next Update: 08/15/26', confirming this is the current, live version of the document as of the capture date.

The preferred-agent identity (zoledronic acid/Reclast as the sole IV bisphosphonate on the market) is standard clinical/drug-class knowledge, not something inferred from this document; the tier, PA-required, and alternatives fields are left null/empty because this pharmacy source states nothing about medical-benefit coverage terms for this class. records.verificationNote: Read directly from the cited Harvard Pilgrim/OptumRx 2026 Select 3-Tier Prescription Drug List, 'Metabolic Bone Disease Agents - Drugs for Osteoporosis' section (document page 135-136). Confirmed verbatim: 'teriparatide solution pen-injector 560 mcg/2.24ml subcutaneous' and the identically-worded all-caps entry 'TERIPARATIDE SOLUTION PEN-INJECTOR 560 MCG/2.24ML SUBCUTANEOUS' are both Tier 3, PA required, QL max 1 pen per 28 days, must use Optum Specialty, not available through home delivery; 'BONSITY SUBCUTANEOUS SOLUTION PEN-INJECTOR 560 MCG/2.24ML' (teriparatide biosimilar) carries identical Tier 3/PA/QL 1-per-28-days terms and is treated as functionally interchangeable with the generic teriparatide entry rather than a separate alternative. 'TYMLOS SUBCUTANEOUS SOLUTION PEN-INJECTOR 3120 MCG/1.56ML' (abaloparatide) is also Tier 3 with PA and QL 1 pen per 30 days -- since it carries its own PA barrier it is listed under paRequired rather than alternatives per the schema's rule that PA-gated drugs never belong in alternatives. Romosozumab (Evenity) does not appear anywhere in the document (checked the full osteoporosis section and searched the entire document text); its absence is flagged as nonformulary rather than inferred coverage. Teriparatide was selected as the preferred agent because it is the only agent in this class with a true generic/biosimilar option (Bonsity), even though PA still applies class-wide; this PA-even-for-preferred fact is captured in preferredRestriction per the task instructions. Document cover page states 'Created: October 1, 2025' with page footers showing 'Last Update: 07/15/26'.

records.verificationNote: Read directly from the cited Harvard Pilgrim/OptumRx 2026 Select 3-Tier Prescription Drug List, 'Hormonal Agents - Selective Estrogen Receptor Modifying Agents' section (document page 116). Confirmed verbatim: 'raloxifene hcl oral tablet 60 mg' is Tier 1 with no PA/ST/QL restriction (HSA-preventive and ACA flags only, which do not gate coverage). 'OSPHENA ORAL TABLET 60 MG' (ospemifene) appears in the same formulary section at Tier 3 with no explicit PA noted, but ospemifene is FDA-indicated for postmenopausal dyspareunia/vulvovaginal atrophy, not osteoporosis, so it was excluded as an osteoporosis-class alternative rather than misrepresented as a covered substitute for raloxifene's bone indication. No other SERM (e.g., bazedoxifene, tamoxifen for osteoporosis) appears in this section. Raloxifene is the only oral SERM FDA-approved and marketed specifically for osteoporosis prevention/treatment, so it is both the sole and preferred agent in this class with clean, unrestricted Tier 1 coverage. Document cover page states 'Created: October 1, 2025' with page footers showing 'Last Update: 07/15/26'.

records.verificationNote: Read directly off Mass General Brigham Health Plan's 2026 6-Tier Pharmacy Formulary (FBO 192 / 20266T.pdf, 135 pages), header stamped 'CURRENT AS OF 7/1/2026'. Confirmed on the alphabetical drug list (PDF page ~7): 'Alendronate Sodium Oral Tablet 10 MG' -> Tier 1, QL 30 EA/30 days; 'Alendronate Sodium Oral Tablet 35 MG' -> Tier 1, QL 4 EA/28 days; 'Alendronate Sodium Oral Tablet 70 MG' (reference: Fosamax) -> Tier 1, QL 4 EA/28 days; oral solution -> Tier 2, no QL. No PA, ST, or SP tag appears on any alendronate line. Risedronate (PDF page ~101: 'Risedronate Sodium Oral Tablet 150 MG' ref Actonel Tier 2 QL 1/28days; '30 MG, 5 MG' Tier 2 QL 30/30days; '35 MG' ref Actonel Tier 2 QL 4/28days; delayed-release ref Atelvia Tier 2 QL 4/28days) and ibandronate (PDF page ~57: 'Ibandronate Sodium Intravenous Solution 3 MG/3ML' Tier 2 no QL; 'Ibandronate Sodium Oral' Tier 2 QL 1/28days) both read directly with no PA/ST flags. Brand names Fosamax/Actonel/Atelvia/Boniva appear only as the 'Reference' cross-index column, not as separately non-formulary or PA-restricted lines -- no brand-only bisphosphonate row exists, consistent with generic-only coverage at these tiers. The 70mg weekly alendronate sig is standard bisphosphonate dosing counsel, not verbatim from the PDF (which lists only tier/QL, not sigs) -- marked verified because the preferred agent, tier, and absence of restrictions were read directly; only the sig wording itself is inferred from standard practice.

Three zoledronic acid lines appear: 'Zoledronic Acid Intravenous Concentrate' -> Tier 5, SP (no strength given, likely the generic concentrate for compounding); 'Zoledronic Acid Intravenous Solution 4 MG/100ML' -> Tier 6, SP (this is the oncology/Zometa-equivalent dose, not the osteoporosis dose); 'Zoledronic Acid Intravenous Solution 5 MG/100ML' with reference 'Reclast' -> Tier 5, SP (this is the once-yearly osteoporosis dose). No PA or ST tag on any zoledronic acid line -- only the SP (specialty) designation. records.verificationNote: Read directly off Mass General Brigham Health Plan's 2026 6-Tier Pharmacy Formulary (FBO 192 / 20266T.pdf), 'CURRENT AS OF 7/1/2026', PDF page ~96 (Prolia) and pages ~27/57/79/118 (biosimilars, alphabetical). 'Prolia Subcutaneous Solution Prefilled Syringe' -> Tier 6, PA; SP; QL (1 SYR per 6 Months) -- confirmed verbatim. Xgeva (denosumab at the oncology 120mg/28-day dose) appears separately at Tier 6, PA; SP; QL (1 VIAL per 28 days) and is a different indication/dose, correctly excluded from this osteoporosis class. Four denosumab biosimilar brand names appear in the document: Conexxence (Tier 6, PA; SP; QL 1 SYR/6 months -- matches Prolia's osteoporosis schedule, listed as an alternative), Bomyntra (Tier 6, PA; SP; QL 1 VIAL or 1 SYR per 28 days -- this dosing interval matches the Xgeva/oncology schedule, not osteoporosis, so it was excluded here), Jubbonti (Tier 5, PA; SP; QL 1 SYR per 28 days -- flagged as an anomaly: Jubbonti is FDA-approved as the Prolia-reference biosimilar and should dose q6months like Prolia/Conexxence, but this PDF's QL note reads 28 days, which matches the Xgeva schedule instead; included as an alternative but the QL text should not be trusted without a follow-up read against the live formulary), and Wyost (Tier 5, PA; SP; QL 1 VIAL per 28 days -- consistent with the Xgeva/oncology reference product, excluded here). Preferred agent, tier, and PA/SP/QL for Prolia itself are verified verbatim; the biosimilar QL inconsistency (Jubbonti) is flagged rather than silently resolved, per this project's convention for PDF table-extraction anomalies.

records.verificationNote: Read directly off Mass General Brigham Health Plan's 2026 6-Tier Pharmacy Formulary (FBO 192 / 20266T.pdf), 'CURRENT AS OF 7/1/2026', PDF page ~111 (teriparatide) and ~117 (Tymlos). Two teriparatide lines both reference brand 'Bonsity': 'Teriparatide Solution Pen-Injector 560 MCG/2.24ML Subcutaneous' and 'Teriparatide Subcutaneous Solution Pen-Injector 560 MCG/2.24ML' -- both Tier 6, PA; SP; ALVOGEN; QL (1 PEN per 28 days), confirmed verbatim (the 'ALVOGEN' note appears to flag a required-manufacturer restriction, i.e., only the Alvogen-manufactured product is covered at this line). 'Bonsity' also appears as its own standalone drug-name entry (PDF page ~16, alphabetical B section) with identical Tier 6, PA; SP; QL (1 PEN per 28 Days). No separate 'Forteo' (the original teriparatide brand) line exists anywhere in the document -- confirmed via full-text search -- meaning Forteo itself is not separately listed/covered; Bonsity (the FDA-approved substitutable teriparatide product) is the only teriparatide formulation on this formulary. 'Tymlos' (abaloparatide) appears once at Tier 6, PA; SP; QL (1 PEN per 30 days), confirmed verbatim, no generic exists for abaloparatide. A full-text search of the entire 135-page document for 'Evenity' and 'romosozumab' (and partial-string variants) returned zero matches -- confirmed via a flattened whitespace-normalized regex search, not just a plain grep, to rule out a mid-word PDF line break. This is treated as a genuine coverage gap (nonformulary) rather than an extraction failure, consistent with the document's exhaustive alphabetical drug-list format where every other osteoporosis anabolic agent (teriparatide, Tymlos) was found without difficulty. Preferred-vs-alternative ordering between teriparatide and Tymlos is inferred (teriparatide chosen as preferred because it has an available generic-equivalent product, Bonsity, while Tymlos has none) since both sit at the identical Tier 6/PA/SP combination with no explicit step-therapy ordering stated in the source.
